With Dean gone and Campbell sidelined, the Eagles have Jeremiah Trotter Jr. and Smael Mondon as linebacker options until Campbell returns.
text:
Eagles linebacker Jihaad Campbell, a full-time starter now that Nakobe Dean is gone, is dealing with a serious shoulder injury that will sideline him for the next several months, defensive coordinator Vic Fangio said Wednesday.

NBC Sports Philadelphia’s John Clark caught up with Fangio at a reception for Hall of Fame coach Dick Vermeil at the Mission Capitalist Club in Whitemarsh Township, Montgomery County.

With Dean signing a three-year contract with the Raiders, Campbell takes over Dean’s starting linebacker spot.

“Well, he's got to be ready,” Fangio said. “And we’ve got to do a good job getting him ready.

“You know, he is dealing with a shoulder injury, he's gonna miss probably most of the offseason. So we're gonna have to cram it into him in training camp. And hopefully we have enough work to get that done for him.”

Campbell has been dealing with shoulder issues since his final year at Alabama. He said he got hurt at some point during the 2024 season and had surgery last March to repair a torn labrum. Injury concerns are likely why Campbell fell to the Eagles with the 31stpick in last year’s draft.

Campbell played in all 18 games last year, starting 10 before Dean returned from rehabbing his knee injury.

But he did appear on the injury report late in the season with what was called a “back/shoulder” injury.

Campbell showed up on the injury list as limited the week before the regular-season finale against Washington, but he was a full participant at practice by Friday and played 62 snaps in the 24-17 loss at the Linc.

But it sounds like he’ll miss all the offseason workouts, including spring OTAs. Considering his lack of experience, that’s important work for any young player. Fangio did seem to think Campbell would be ready to go for training camp in late July.

With Dean gone and Campbell sidelined, the Eagles have Jeremiah Trotter Jr. and Smael Mondon as linebacker options until Campbell returns.

Fangio also spoke about the free agency losses of Dean, Reed Blankenship and Jaelan Phillips from last year’s defense.

“Yeah, it's like we're the farm system defensively for the 31 other teams in the league,” he said. “But we'll do a good job. Howie does a great job putting together the roster. The assistant coaches will do a great job getting the new guys ready, and we'll be ready.

“But we'll miss them. You know, Nikobe had a great two years for us. You know, We're going to miss him. I really enjoyed the guy.

“We lost Reid. Reid was a great story, coming from an undrafted free agent to being a starter, winning a Super Bowl, being an elected captain. You know, he's got a lot of intangibles besides his good play that we'll miss.

“And Jaelen Phillips, he’s a hell of a player, as you saw Carolina thought so, too, with the amount of money they paid him. We were trying hard to keep him, but it got a bit too expensive. And I wish him nothing but the best of luck.”

The one addition on defense is outside forner Riq Woolen, a Pro Bowler in 2022. Woolen’s 12 interceptions since 2022 are 10th-most in the NFL and 4th-most among cornerbacks. He joins Quinyon Mitchell and Cooper DeJean in a loaded cornerback room.

“I haven’t met him yet, I only know him from video study and evaluating him,” Fangio said. “And we're hopeful that he's going to come in here and play very well. If Tariq can turn out to be what we hope he can be, it will be three good cover guys.”

Fangio goes back to the early 1980s with Vermeil. They first crossed paths when Fangio was coaching at his alma mater, Dunmore High outside Scranton.

“When I first started coaching in high school, I went to a clinic where Dick spoke at,” he said. “It was down in Atlantic City, and he was the coach of the Eagles at the time. So I was fired up to go see him, and I even went and got his autograph after he spoke. And I told him that story, and now this many years later, we're good friends. We text before and after every game, text in the offseason, been to each other's house.

“And, you know, to go from that clinic where I was a high school coach, and he was the head coach of the Eagles to now, what a great relationship I have with him. You know, it means a lot.

title: Green Bay Packers Re-sign Key Tight End Depth Piece In 2026 NFL Free Agency Amid ...
description:
The Green Bay Packers may not be massive players in NFL free agency this year.
One of the players that Green Bay wants to extend is tight end Tucker Kraft.
The Green Bay Packers re-sign Josh Whyle amid Tucker Kraft uncertainty Kraft was well on his way to having the best season ever for a Packers tight end when he went down last season.
Without him and Kraft, Luke Musgrave was the only real piece of tight end depth that the Packers had.
In very limited playing time last season, Whyle caught five passes for 36 yards and a touchdown.
text:
The Green Bay Packers may not be massive players in NFL free agency this year. After two seasons of making significant additions (with mixed results) on the free agent market, the prevailing belief is that the Packers are going to invest most of their salary cap resources into contract extensions for several of their own players.

That is not to say that they will not sign any free agents. In fact, they already agreed to a two-year contract with cornerback Benjamin St-Juste. However, fans should not expect them to target any of the bigger names on the market.

One of the players that Green Bay wants to extend is tight end Tucker Kraft. However, they may be waiting to see how he looks when he comes back from his torn ACL.

The Green Bay Packers re-sign Josh Whyle amid Tucker Kraft uncertainty

Kraft was well on his way to having the best season ever for a Packers tight end when he went down last season. In just eight games, he had 32 receptions for 489 receiving yards and six touchdowns. He still finished tied for the team lead in touchdown receptions.

The hope, of course, is that he is able to come back better than ever after his torn ACL. That being said, the team is not sure if he will be able to be ready for the start of the season.

As an insurance policy in the event that he can’t, they have re-signed tight end Josh Whyle, according to Bill Huber of Sports Illustrated:

“It’s a one-year deal for Whyle, who was scheduled to be a restricted free agent. He will provide critical depth at a position in which Tucker Kraft is coming off a torn ACL and John FitzPatrick is a free agent and coming off a torn Achilles.”

As Huber notes, John Fitzpatrick, also a tight end, is not going to play at all in 2026 after suffering a torn Achilles. Without him and Kraft, Luke Musgrave was the only real piece of tight end depth that the Packers had.

With Whyle on the roster again, they will hopefully be in a good position, especially if/when Kraft comes back.

In very limited playing time last season, Whyle caught five passes for 36 yards and a touchdown. He was originally claimed by the Packers off of waivers from the Tennessee Titans. They signed him to the active roster when Kraft got hurt.

text:
The Houston Texans have agreed to a contract with free agent tight end Foster Moreau, according to NFL Network.

The move adds depth to the Texans' tight end room, which features Dalton Schultz as the starter.

Moreau, 28, played in 11 games for the New Orleans Saints in 2025 after starting the season on the physically unable to perform list. Moreau was coming off a knee injury that occurred in the 2024 season finale. He caught seven passes for 59 yards last season and missed the final two games of the season due to an ankle injury that was not considered a long-term issue.

The Saints added veteran tight end Noah Fant in the free agent negotiating period.

Moreau has spent the last three seasons in New Orleans after signing with them as a free agent in 2023. He spent the first four seasons of his career with the Raiders, who selected him out of LSU in the fourth round of the 2019 draft. He tied a career-high of five receiving touchdowns in 2024 prior to his injury.

He briefly stepped away from football in the spring of 2023 after he was diagnosed with Hodgkin lymphoma following a physical on a visit with the Saints. He announced that his cancer was in full remission in July 2023.

He has played in 104 games and has caught 151 passes for 1,722 yards and 18 touchdowns in seven seasons.

title: Ten takeaways from Day 1 of NFL free agency, including new-look Chiefs, uneventful Cowboys
description:
The Athletic has live coverage of the 2026 NFL free agency.
Inside: 10 things you should know from the first day of free agency, plus the big names still available.
Free agency: What we learned on day one Over $2 billion in free-agency contracts were agreed to yesterday, so I understand if your head is spinning.
Since 2024, Kansas City running backs have ranked 31st in yards per carry and 32nd in yards after contact per run.
Extra Points 🎙 Live breakdowns of free agency all week on “The Athletic Football Show,” starting at 3 p.m.
text:
The Athletic has live coverage of the 2026 NFL free agency.

Inside: 10 things you should know from the first day of free agency, plus the big names still available. Let’s go.

This article is from Scoop City, The Athletic’s NFL newsletter. Sign up here to receive it directly in your inbox.

Free agency: What we learned on day one

Over $2 billion in free-agency contracts were agreed to yesterday, so I understand if your head is spinning. Before we get into the 10 moves that foreshadow what’s to come in 2026, here’s how The Athletic graded the highest-paid players:

Onto the list:

1. Same old Cowboys? Jerry Jones promised Dallas would spend more than usual in this free-agency period. Naturally, not much of that has happened. The Cowboys did add two serviceable starters, but much more is needed to fix one of the worst defenses in franchise history.

Having recently freed $66 million in cap space, they could still make a splash. The biggest name remaining? Only the No. 1 overall free agent, edge rusher Trey Hendrickson, reportedly seeking over $30 million. 👀

2. Should we let the Dolphins fast forward to 2027? They landed quarterback Malik Willis with a reasonable deal averaging $22.5 million for three years, slightly above what the Jets gave Justin Fields last offseason. Two years guaranteed means it’s essentially a short-term trial for the promising 26-year-old, who joins De’Von Achane and Jaylen Waddle in what should again be the league’s fastest offense.

They might want to sprint past this season. After cutting Tua Tagovailoa, who signed with the Falcons, over half of Miami’s 2026 salary cap is going to players not on the roster. $151 million-plus in dead money!!

3. Expect Jeremiyah Love to be drafted between No. 4 and 7. The Notre Dame running back is a Jahmyr Gibbs-level prospect in a draft devoid of true difference-makers. Love’s landing spots seem to have narrowed to the Titans at No. 4 or Commanders at No. 7, after the holders of picks Nos. 3, 8 and 9 added running backs:

The Cardinals (No. 3) added former Falcons backup Tyler Allgeier and retained James Conner.

The Saints (No. 8) agreed to terms with Travis Etienne yesterday, which could spell the end for Alvin Kamara’s time in New Orleans.

The Chiefs (No. 9) evidently didn’t think Love would fall to them, which brings me to …

4. Kansas City’s new-look offense. Signing the Super Bowl MVP was a wise decision for the Chiefs, whose run game struggled across the past two seasons. Since 2024, Kansas City running backs have ranked 31st in yards per carry and 32nd in yards after contact per run.

“If we can be more explosive and more effective in the running game, we can take a lot of pressure off (Patrick Mahomes), especially coming back early in the season with the injury,” Kansas City general manager Brett Veach said in February.

Enter Walker, an explosive athlete who had more 20-plus-yard runs than every Chiefs running back combined last season (12 to one if we add playoffs), including this in the Super Bowl:

The 25-year-old finished fifth in explosive run rate last season and was the highest-ranked free-agent running back available. A perfect upgrade, I’d say. He’ll work under the returning Eric Bieniemy to help lead a newly physical Chiefs offense.

5. Mike Evans’ new digs. The 49ers brought in the 12-year veteran with a three-year, $20 million-per offer, one filled with incentives. How does he respond after his first truly injury-plagued season?

A dominant, six-catch, 132-yard Week 15 suggests there’s still something in the tank as he enters his age-33 season. The 6-foot-5 receiver is a strong fit for Kyle Shanahan’s offense as a man-coverage beater, offering Brock Purdy a towering red zone target and deep threat.

6. You won’t recognize the Raiders. On Sunday night, Las Vegas had one player making over $10 million. They added seven yesterday, and general manager John Spytek and new coach Klint Kubiak made (mostly) savvy moves, as graded by my colleagues Mike Jones and Daniel Popper.

Most notable was center Tyler Linderbaum, whose $27 million average was a staggering 50 percent jump above the next highest-paid center, Kansas City’s Creed Humphrey ($18M). Unheard of, but don’t call it an overpay for the three-time Pro Bowler. Linderbaum offers veteran leadership, protecting an expected rookie quarterback and clearing lanes for Ashton Jeanty.

7. The Panthers are here to stay. After their first playoff appearance in seven years, Carolina just added two of the top three overall free agents: Eagles edge Jaelan Phillips and Jaguars linebacker Devin Lloyd. Those additions weren’t cheap — $20 million per year for Phillips and $15 million for Lloyd — but add enormous value to a suddenly ascending defense that has the makings of a top-10 unit.

8. The Rams are self-aware. Seattle exposed their weakness in the NFC title game, when Sam Darnold threw for 346 yards and three touchdowns against an overmatched secondary.

That didn’t sit well with general manager Les Snead. He’s since traded a first-round pick and change to make Trent McDuffie the league’s highest-paid corner, then paired the former Chief with a Kansas City teammate, corner Jaylen Watson. Add the return of nickel Quentin Lake, and this cornerback room is suddenly a strength. Well done.

9. The Jets defense improved. Last year, we ranked all 32 teams by how successful they were at signing free agents. Guess who finished last?

Hint: That same team just addressed significant needs via free agency, adding leadership with the seemingly ageless, 37-year-old linebacker Demario Davis ($11 million per year) and pass rush depth with two mid-level edge rushers. Also adding veteran safety Minkah Fitzpatrick and a potential pass rusher at No. 2, this historically bad defense could make a significant jump.

10. Even Cincinnati’s defense got better. The Bengals are adding promising edge Boye Mafe (three years, $20 million per) and safety Bryan Cook (three years, $13.5 million per), quality defenders who should improve a unit that had the league’s most missed tackles last season.

As our Paul Dehner Jr. explains, “These were legitimate top-of-the-market spends at two critical positions. Depending on what happens at linebacker, they could add a top-25 overall free agent to each level of the defense.”

Lastly, keep an eye on undervalued signings. The Buccaneers added Lions linebacker Alex Anzalone, while the Chargers landed run-blocking tight end Charlie Kolar from Baltimore. They were both A+ signings, per those grades by Jones and Popper.

What does it mean for your fantasy team? We broke down each offseason move here. Kenneth Walker stock 📈 📈.

Who’s still available?

While Cincinnati’s 31-year-old star pass rusher remains available, Hendrickson isn’t the only big name still negotiating. Each of these prominent players could still be strong additions (age as of 2026’s Week 1):

QB Kyler Murray (29). His salary will be irrelevant here, since he is due $36.8 million from the Cardinals and his next contract will just reduce what they owe him. The Browns, Jets and Vikings are potential landing spots for 2019’s No. 1 pick.

WR Tyreek Hill (32). He was still an elite receiver prior to last season’s knee injury, ranking No. 4 in yards per route run. Expect an incentive-laden deal, reducing his next team’s risk. Kansas City return?

QB Aaron Rodgers (42). The Steelers’ swing for receiver Michael Pittman should help entice Rodgers to return to Pittsburgh, as they’ve finally added a legitimate No. 2 across from DK Metcalf.

While those are the best-known players, none ranks among the 10 best still available. For those, read our full list of the top remaining free agents.

Extra Points

🎙 Live breakdowns of free agency all week on “The Athletic Football Show,” starting at 3 p.m. ET. It’ll be on in my house all week. You can listen here.

📋 Draft order. The NFL released 33 compensatory picks for the 2026 draft, with the Eagles, Steelers and 49ers each landing multiple top-140 picks.

📃 Patriots get risky. New England agreed to a deal with the often-injured, high-potential guard Alijah Vera-Tucker. The 26-year-old missed the Jets’ entire 2025 season with a torn bicep and has averaged eight games played per season, but is a strong option when healthy.

📊 Seahawks encore? It helps that receiver/returner Rashid Shaheed is coming back, though Seattle has lost plenty of talent. Michael-Shawn Dugar recaps the Seahawks’ first day here.

▶️ Yesterday’s most-clicked: Let’s not forget Rondale Moore so quickly.

title: Giants' Cam Skattebo Speaks Out on Injury Recovery & Timeline - Heavy Sports
description:
Fortunately for the Giants, Skattebo is making significant progress in his injury rehab, as he revealed on The Rich Eisen Show this week.
Connor Hughes of SNY believes wide receiver Brian Thomas Jr. could be a trade target for the Giants.
“I could see the Giants potentially getting available because they made calls on him as well is Brian Thomas of the Jaguars is available,” Hughes said.
“He is somebody the Jaguars are entertaining offers to.” Former Giants star running back Tiki Barber thinks Thomas would be an “interesting” addition alongside Nabers.
Brian Thomas is a really good wide receiver.
text:
The New York Giants have undergone drastic changes after finishing the 2025 NFL season with a disappointing 4-13 record, securing the No. 5 overall pick in the 2026 NFL Draft.

Along with bringing in one-time Super Bowl champion head coach John Harbaugh to replace Brian Daboll, the Giants have a bright future ahead, with a rising star quarterback under center in Jaxson Dart, one of the most talented young wide receivers downfield in Malik Nabers and an up-and-coming running back in Cam Skattebo.

Giants Star Cam Skattebo Gives an Injury Update

Even though the future is bright in the Big Apple, with one of the best head coaches in the NFL leading the way, two of the team’s young stars are working their way back from season-ending injuries, as Nabers suffered a torn ACL and Skattebo went down with a gruesome ankle injury.

Fortunately for the Giants, Skattebo is making significant progress in his injury rehab, as he revealed on The Rich Eisen Show this week.

“I’m getting better,” Skattebo said. “I ran for the first time today about 75%, so it’s the bright side of things.”

Eisen asked Skattebo how fast he could run at 75 percent, and Skattebo gave a hilarious, classic response that only he would give.

“Not very fast, I’ll tell you that. … I can get away from like a baby hippo maybe, but that’s about it. A little bit over a jog. Kind of just striding out, not exploding yet and getting into that part of the game, but the jog and the striding.”

After giving an update on his injury rehab, Skattebo let it be known that he will be ready to take the field once the Giants’ training camp gets underway.

“I’ll be ready to go by training camp,” Skattebo said. “I probably got a little over a month left till I’m fully back to 100%. So OTAs are coming up in about a month and I’m excited. I’ll be ready to go.”

Although he’s still got some rehabbing to do, he’s clearly headed in the right direction, which is great news for the Giants.

Giants Legend Tiki Barber Chimes in on Brian Thomas Jr.

With Wan’Dale Robinson agreeing to sign with the Tennessee Titans, reuniting him with Daboll, the Giants’ brass will likely be looking for a viable wide receiver to bring in to join Nabers and Darius Slayton.

Connor Hughes of SNY believes wide receiver Brian Thomas Jr. could be a trade target for the Giants.

“I could see the Giants potentially getting available because they made calls on him as well is Brian Thomas of the Jaguars is available,” Hughes said. “He is somebody the Jaguars are entertaining offers to.”

Former Giants star running back Tiki Barber thinks Thomas would be an “interesting” addition alongside Nabers.

“That sounds interesting to me,” Barber said of the Giants’ potential pursuit of Thomas. “You bring Malik Nabers back with his college teammate. Brian Thomas is a really good wide receiver. He’s had injury issues, so that’s the one thing you worry about.”

Only time will tell what the Giants’ brass choose to do, but they’ll have some options to explore, including trading for Thomas.

text:
The Seattle Seahawks have re-signed safety AJ Finley, according to Finley’s agency, SportsTrust Advisors.

Seattle Seahawks Offseason Tracker: Free agents, draft, more

Finley, a 24-year-old restricted free agent, was a candidate to earn a backup safety role for the Seahawks this past season, but landed on injured reserve on Aug. 4 with a knee injury suffered in practice and missed the rest of the season.

Finley, who went undrafted out of Ole Miss in 2023, was claimed off waivers by the Seahawks on Nov. 26, 2024. He appeared in four of Seattle’s final six games that season, recording one tackle while playing six snaps on defense and 62 on special teams.

A Mobile, Ala., native, Finley originally signed with the Los Angeles Chargers in 2023. He had three tackles and a forced fumble over 12 games during his rookie season, which included playing 200 snaps on special teams.

Finley appeared in 10 games for Los Angeles in 2024 before he was released on Nov. 25. He had 11 tackles while continuing to get the majority of his snaps on special teams.

Finley was a second-team All-SEC selection as junior at Ole Miss in 2021.

title: Maxx Crosby's wife reacts to Ravens trade fallout with three-word message - Fox News
description:
On Tuesday, the Ravens pulled out of a trade that would have brought the star pass rusher to Baltimore for multiple draft picks.
"The Baltimore Ravens have backed out of our trade agreement for Maxx Crosby," the Raiders said in a statement.
The sudden reversal drew a sharp response from Crosby’s wife, Rachel, who defended her husband and pushed back on claims about his knee recovery.
Despite learning his knee would require offseason surgery, Crosby continued to play through the injury, according to reports.
He also acknowledged the challenges the Ravens' medical staff face in assessing future risk, particularly given Crosby’s stage in the recovery process.
text:
NEWYou can now listen to Fox News articles!

Maxx Crosby's farewell to the Las Vegas Raiders may have been premature. On Tuesday, the Ravens pulled out of a trade that would have brought the star pass rusher to Baltimore for multiple draft picks.

"The Baltimore Ravens have backed out of our trade agreement for Maxx Crosby," the Raiders said in a statement. "We will have no further comment at this time." ESPN, citing league sources, reported that the Ravens decision centered around medical concerns that surfaced during Crosby's physical. NFL trades hinge on players clearing a physical.

The sudden reversal drew a sharp response from Crosby’s wife, Rachel, who defended her husband and pushed back on claims about his knee recovery.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

In an Instagram Stories post, Rachel shared a tweet from Crosby’s agent stating the defensive end remains "on track" in his recovery from a knee injury.

"Maxx continues to be on track in his recovery and if anything is ahead of schedule according to his surgeon Dr. Neal ElAttrache," C.J. LaBoy wrote. "Maxx remains on track to return during the offseason program & will undoubtedly return as the dominant game wrecker he has been these past 7 seasons."

JETS TO REUNITE WITH GENO SMITH AFTER RAIDERS GAIN DRAFT PICK IN TRADE: REPORTS

Rachel echoed LaBoy’s comments, adding "Say it louder," along with a pair of clapping emojis. The NFL star later shared the tweet on his social media platform.

Crosby had surgery in January to address the torn meniscus in his left knee. Despite learning his knee would require offseason surgery, Crosby continued to play through the injury, according to reports. The Raiders shut down Crosby during the final stretch of the 2025 season, a move that appeared to frustrate the five-time Pro Bowler.

Dr. Neal ElAttrache, Crosby’s surgeon, offered an update last week on the 28-year-old’s recovery.

"Maxx Crosby is doing very well in the early part of his rehab and recovery from surgery to treat a significant meniscus tear and the related stress injury to the bone and cartilage in his knee. He is certainly on track in his planned program….he feels very well and is already much improved compared to before surgery," ElAttrache told ESPN.

He also acknowledged the challenges the Ravens' medical staff face in assessing future risk, particularly given Crosby’s stage in the recovery process.

"We truly respect the work of the Ravens staff with Maxx regarding the contract physical exam. We also understand the challenge the staff faces when tasked to provide a future risk assessment based on an evaluation early in the recovery process. This is especially challenging when dealing with an elite player like Maxx considering the level of commitment necessary for a team to obtain him. The timing of this assessment is unfortunate because the apparent risk will lessen as his recovery progresses and his return to performance over the next few months becomes clear."

CLICK HERE TO GET THE FOX NEWS APP

Crosby and the Raiders reached an agreement on the three-year extension last March.

text:
New York Giants fans quickly attached to the team's rookie duo, quarterback Jaxson Dart and running back Cam Skattebo, last season. Dart and Skattebo frequently went viral for their headbutt celebration, and their bruising style of play captured league-wide attention.

The dawn of the Dawn and Skattebo era in New York hit its apex when the Giants upset the then-defending champion Philadelphia Eagles 34-17 on Thursday, Oct. 9. Skattebo went off for 98 yards and three touchdowns, while Dart accounted for 253 yards and two touchdowns — 195 yards and one touchdown through the air, 58 yards and one touchdown on the ground.

Unfortunately, the Giants' momentum was derailed when they met the Eagles again on Oct. 26. Skattebo suffered a heinous season-ending injury. According to ESPN's Jordan Raanan, he dislocated his ankle, suffered an open tibia fracture, and ruptured his deltoid ligament.

On Wednesday, Skattebo joined The Rich Eisen Show and provided an update on his rehabilitation.

"I'm getting better," the 24-year-old said. "I ran for the first time today at about 75%, so it's the bright side of things."

When Eisen asked what 75% looked like, Skattebo said, "Not very fast, I'll tell you that. I can get away from, like, a baby hippo, maybe. That's about it. A little bit over a job. Kind of just striding out, not exploding yet, and getting into that part of the game, but the jog and the striding."

Skattebo said the only thing slowing him down is the nature of his injury, but he'll "absolutely" be ready for training camp in July. "I've probably got a little over a month left until I'm fully back to 100%," he said. "OTAs are coming up in about a month, and I'm excited. I'll be ready to go."

Skattebo also provided details from his initial conversations with newly hired Giants head coach John Harbaugh, who coached the Baltimore Ravens from 2008 until last season. "He told me he respects the way I run the ball and the offense that he's implementing is downhill running, and it's perfect for what I am," he said.

Skattebo finished his rookie campaign with 410 yards and five touchdowns on 101 carries and 4.1 yards per attempt in eight games.

Skattebo's comments echo what SNY's Connor Hughes reported earlier this month after the 2026 NFL Combine.

"Harbaugh has a clear vision for the team’s offensive identity: run the ball — and then run it again — behind a dominant offensive line," Hughes wrote. "That makes the addition of Greg Roman to the coaching staff particularly notable."

text:
In their first order of business to kick off the new league year, the Detroit Lions released defensive end Josh Paschal.

Paschal spent the entire 2025 season on the nonfootball injury last after undergoing back surgery. His contract tolled for 2026 and the Lions could have kept him on their roster, but general manager Brad Holmes was noncommittal about Paschal's future in late February at the NFL combine.

"Josh, we’ll have the ability to bring him back," Holmes said. "There's still discussions to be had about that one, but yeah, it's a possibility."

A second-round pick at No. 46 overall out of Kentucky in 2022, Paschal had five sacks while playing as a rotational defensive end and part-time starter in his first three NFL seasons, when he missed 15 games with an assortment of ailments.

The Lions remain in the market for defensive line help after failing to land a pass rusher during the free agent negotiating period.

Aidan Hutchinson and Ahmed Hassanein are the top defensive ends on the roster, though Levi Onwuzurike – whose contract also tolled after he missed the year following knee surgery – also can play the position. Hassanein, a sixth-round pick last year, did not play in 2025.

Al-Quadin Muhammad, who finished second on the Lions with a career-high 11 sacks, and Marcus Davenport are free agents.

The Lions have added four players in free agency so far, agreeing to deals with offensive linemen Cade Mays and Larry Borom, quarterback Teddy Bridgewater and running back Isiah Pacheco, and agreed to new deals with cornerback Rock Ya-Sin and linebacker Malcolm Rodriguez.

They've lost four contributors in Alex Anzalone, Amik Robertson, Roy Lopez and Kalif Raymond, plus backup quarterback Kyle Allen.

title: Warriors star Steph Curry to miss more time due to knee injury, to be re-evaluated in 10 days
description:
SAN FRANCISCO — Golden State Warriors star Stephen Curry will miss at least six more games as he continues to deal with a lingering runner’s knee injury and will be re-evaluated in 10 days, the team said Wednesday.
Those workouts will continue to “intensify” as long as Curry’s knee continues to feel better while he ramps up his rehab.
He has missed the last 15 games and is now on track to miss at least six more.
The good news for the Warriors is that six of their last 17 games are against tanking and/or bad teams.
The return of Curry, who is averaging 27.2 points this season, can’t come soon enough for the Warriors.
text:
SAN FRANCISCO — Golden State Warriors star Stephen Curry will miss at least six more games as he continues to deal with a lingering runner’s knee injury and will be re-evaluated in 10 days, the team said Wednesday.

The Warriors said that Curry, who turns 38 on Saturday, is making “good progress” in his return and has started to do some individual on-court work. Those workouts will continue to “intensify” as long as Curry’s knee continues to feel better while he ramps up his rehab.

Curry has not played since leaving a loss to the Detroit Pistons early on Jan. 30. He has missed the last 15 games and is now on track to miss at least six more.

The hope within the organization was that the two-time MVP would be able to return after the All-Star break, but that didn’t happen as he continued to deal with pain in his knee. The Warriors have gone 5-10 in Curry’s absence and have slid to ninth in the Western Conference standings, 1 1/2 games in front of the Portland Trail Blazers. Their next game is Friday at home against the Minnesota Timberwolves, after which they embark on a six-game East Coast road trip beginning Sunday night at the New York Knicks.

The reality for the Warriors (32-33) is that even if Curry can return soon, their place among Play-In teams is unlikely to change in either direction. They are now six games behind the seventh-place Phoenix Suns with 17 games left to play. It’s a place that Warriors coach Steve Kerr and his team have been resigned to for the last couple of weeks as they have held out hope that Curry can return.

The Warriors know that they always have a chance to win when Curry is on the floor, and are not yet seriously considering the possibility that he could be ruled out for the season. Curry still wants to play — as evidenced by the work he is putting in behind the scenes — and the team would at least like to get a look at how he looks playing alongside new acquisition Kristaps Porzingis.

Beyond that, as the standings illustrate, only tanking and/or bad teams remain behind the Blazers in the standings. There’s only so far the Warriors could drop, even if they wanted to. The Memphis Grizzlies, currently in 11th place in the West, are 23-41 and have already made deals to ensure they can continue losing down the stretch.

Ideally, the Warriors’ front office would like Curry to come back, see if they can win a few postseason games, and provide a little momentum heading into the offseason. But given that the Warriors just lost back-to-back games to the tanking Utah Jazz and Chicago Bulls, it is clear that they have to play almost perfectly without Curry to beat any team.

The good news for the Warriors is that six of their last 17 games are against tanking and/or bad teams. In the short term, as Curry continues to be out, look for Kerr to continue to rely on a trifecta of guards in Brandin Podziemski, Pat Spencer and LJ Cryer to help set up the offense. As Kerr has shown in recent games, he will go to whichever one of the three is playing well at the moment.

The return of Curry, who is averaging 27.2 points this season, can’t come soon enough for the Warriors. But even the magic he provides on the floor won’t be enough to lift them out of the standings predicament they find themselves in.

title: Late for Work: Pundits Look at Why the Maxx Crosby Trade Fell Through - Baltimore Ravens
description:
"They wanted it all to happen, and when it seemed like it was falling apart, they still tried to make it work.
They also may have found more arthritis in the knee, at which point Crosby's longevity would become a bigger question.
They were unified in the belief that Crosby's short-term outlook was positive, but there were concerns about the long-term prognosis of Crosby's knee, per league sources.
Hall, who failed a physical; the Texans and Seattle Seahawks amended a 2017 trade that was going to include cornerback Jeremy Lane.
Any concerns emanating from the physical are brought forward to the front office and ultimately factor into the team's final decision regarding the trade."
text:
"They wanted it all to happen, and when it seemed like it was falling apart, they still tried to make it work. They just were not comfortable with the medical. They were not comfortable that the player they believed they were acquiring was going to be at a point where he could play to the level that they all thought. It all sucks."

Sports Illustrated’s Albert Breer: "My understanding is that the Ravens were not comfortable with the condition of Crosby's knee — in which he had a meniscus repair — after Tuesday's physical. So, what did the Ravens discover during the physical, or in the imaging they did themselves, that wasn't in the information the Raiders passed along to them during the normal course of the trade? That's the question. … Well, not all meniscus repairs heal properly, and the chance one wouldn't heal would go up as a player gets older. They also may have found more arthritis in the knee, at which point Crosby's longevity would become a bigger question. Either way, teams interested before Crosby was traded did have concerns about the toll his workload and breakneck playing style might've taken on him. And a meniscus injury can result from wear and tear."

The Athletic’s Jeff Zrebiec, Dianna Russini, and Vic Tafur: "Teams that were interested in trading for Crosby, including the Ravens, had conversations with his surgeon, Neal ElAttrache, and the Raiders' medical staff before making trade offers. The Ravens' doctors — along with multiple independent ones — reviewed Crosby's MRI. They were unified in the belief that Crosby's short-term outlook was positive, but there were concerns about the long-term prognosis of Crosby's knee, per league sources. Baltimore believed the risk was too high given the compensation it was giving up."

CBS Sports’ Jonathan Jones: "Sources at multiple teams reached out to CBS Sports Tuesday night to say they had concerns about Crosby's knee during their own evaluation process. A Raiders source during Crosby's tenure with the team said he struggled to get through light practices at times due to knee and ankle issues."

ESPN’s Kevin Seifert: "The bottom line is that no trade, and no player transaction of any kind, is final until everyone involved passes a physical. In the NFL, there is no uniform standard for evaluating the results of a physical. Each team's decision-makers, in consultation with medical staff, use the information to make a projection about any potential health risks. In almost every instance, the physical is a formality. But there is recent precedent for NFL teams canceling trades over health concerns.

"Three recent examples include: The Houston Texans backed out of a 2022 trade for Miami Dolphins tight end Adam Shaheen because of concerns about a preexisting knee injury; the Minnesota Vikings called off a trade in 2020 for Raiders defensive tackle P.J. Hall, who failed a physical; the Texans and Seattle Seahawks amended a 2017 trade that was going to include cornerback Jeremy Lane. When Lane failed his physical, the Seahawks added draft-pick compensation to complete a deal that brought them left tackle Duane Brown."

ESPN injury analyst Stephania Bell: "The bar is set internally by the organization, as every deal is a value proposition of risk versus reward. When a unique, high-value talent is the potential acquisition, risk tolerance becomes a factor. Crosby's hefty contract and two first-round picks make for a high price and the Ravens would merely be doing their due diligence in leaving no stone unturned during the screening process. Trade physicals are akin to NFL combine screenings, where any previous injuries -- even if the player recovered to return to his previous level of play -- might warrant further evaluation, including imaging. The physical is conducted not only to evaluate the athlete at that moment in time, but also in consideration of his long-term playing health. Any concerns emanating from the physical are brought forward to the front office and ultimately factor into the team's final decision regarding the trade."

title: The Ravens voiding the Maxx Crosby trade needs an NFL investigation | SB Nation
description:
The Baltimore Ravens sent shockwaves through the NFL on Tuesday night with the news that they failed Maxx Crosby’s during his post-trade physical and were voiding their deal with the Raiders.
What is Maxx Crosby’s injury?
Teams around the NFL knew the details of this injury before Crosby hit the trade market.
Essentially if he says that the surgery went well and that Maxx Crosby was ahead of his rehabilitation timeline, then it might as well be taken as gospel.
This extended into the second with updates that Hendrickson was lowering his asking price — then, shortly after came the news that the Ravens were voiding their Crosby trade.
text:
The Baltimore Ravens sent shockwaves through the NFL on Tuesday night with the news that they failed Maxx Crosby’s during his post-trade physical and were voiding their deal with the Raiders. As a result the two first round picks they dealt went back to the Ravens, Crosby returned to Las Vegas, and GMs around the league were accusing Baltimore of dirty pool.

It was a move that came under even more scrutiny on Wednesday morning when the Ravens rushed to sign Trey Hendrickson, the best remaining edge rusher on the market. It made the whole situation appear as though the decision to fail Crosby was far more due to the organization getting cold feet, rather than any actual concern about Crosby’s medical history.

We’re left with more questions than answers about the Ravens’ approach to the trade, their actions after it, and whether or not the NFL needs take action to close loopholes surrounding failed physicals. Baltimore is doing its best to portray this as normal, but what they did on Tuesday night is anything but commonplace.

What is Maxx Crosby’s injury?

In October of 2025 the pass rusher sustained a meniscus injury that caused him to miss several games while the Raiders waited to see if the injury would heal on its own, which is the common first step for these types of injuries.

Sometime before January it was decided that Crosby’s meniscus would not heal without surgical intervention, leading to meniscus repair surgery, which was performed successfully in January of 2026. This involved arthroscopic surgery to enter the knee joint and suture together ruptured pieces of the meniscus, with the goal to be to return the knee to normal function.

Crosby is in the process of rehabilitation, with agent CJ LaBoy saying that Maxx was ahead of schedule on his recovery, meaning he would be back in time for Week 1 of the 2026 season.

What did the rest of the league know?

Everything, according to NFL Insiders, who have been following the story. Teams around the NFL knew the details of this injury before Crosby hit the trade market. There was no subterfuge on the part of the Raiders, or efforts to hide the severity of the knee injury. A meniscus tear is unquestionably serious, but barely a blip on the radar when it comes to most NFL injuries, with the vast majority of players making a full recovery with no risk of further injury, setting it apart from other common injuries like torn ligaments.

In addition, the surgery was performed by Dr. Neal ElAttrache, one of the most respected and highly-regarded orthopedic sports surgeons in the world. Dr. ElAttrache is a foremost expert on these types of injuries, and he also serves as the team doctor for the Los Angeles Rams and Dodgers, handling sports medicine for both organizations.

Essentially if he says that the surgery went well and that Maxx Crosby was ahead of his rehabilitation timeline, then it might as well be taken as gospel.

What did the Ravens see and why is this unusual?

That’s the $36 million dollar question here.

At the heart of the voided trade were vague “concerns” over Crosby’s knee. Information about Crosby failing the Ravens’ physical hasn’t been released, nor are either team at liberty to discuss the medical side of the issue further.

While voided trades happen fairly routinely in the NFL, it’s always due to an undisclosed or underlying condition that wasn’t reported by the team trading a player. This is often attributed to missing an injury, rather than any underlying malice. It’s unheard of to have a team void a trade based on previously known information, especially when the surgical doctor has signed off on the recovery timetable.

Ostensibly, it’s the Ravens saying that their medical staff is better equipped to make a diagnosis on Crosby than one of the most renowned orthopedic surgeons in the world. As a result Crosby has been labeled as a potential injury concern, it’s unclear if any teams will re-engage trade discussions with the Raiders, and Las Vegas has no way to seek compensation for what Baltimore did in voiding the trade.

The timing of all this raises serious questions about the Ravens’ motives

When the trade was initially agreed to it came with the tacit understanding that the Ravens were getting value when it came to the salary cap hit from Maxx Crosby. Sure, he was one of the highest-paid pass rushers in the NFL, but with the expanding salary cap came the realization that contracts were going to get larger.

This assumption was accurate, with Crosby’s $35.79M cap hit being lower than that of Jaelan Phillips, who signed with the Panthers in free agency. It was also reported that free agent Trey Hendrickson was seeking a contract close to that of Micah Parsons, which is paying the Packers edge $46.5M per year.

Reports emerged that Hendrickson’s camp was asking for too much money when they couldn’t reach a deal during the first day of tampering. This extended into the second with updates that Hendrickson was lowering his asking price — then, shortly after came the news that the Ravens were voiding their Crosby trade.

Less than 12 hours later Baltimore signed Hendrickson to a four-year, $112M deal that would pay him $28M AAV, almost $8M less than what Crosby was set to earn.

We’re left with a curiously failed physical without much information, and a team that appears as though it simply regretted offering two first-round picks for Maxx Crosby after coming to the realization they could just sign Trey Hendrickson for less money. Then there’s the Raiders, who have no mechanism to get restitution from the Ravens.

What does this do to the Ravens and Raiders?

The Ravens effectively get to move on like nothing happened. They get back their draft picks, pivoted to Hendrickson, and opened up more cap space to make future moves.

This dramatically damages the Las Vegas Raiders, who functionally had their offseason turned on its head. The team is fine in terms of cap space, because they had ample room to make more deals. However, it changes their team-building structure. Would Las Vegas have signed Kwity Paye to a $48M contact? Would the Raiders have re-signed Malcolm Koonce for $10M this year if they knew Crosby would be coming back? Would the team have prioritized getting a better wide receiver had they known they wouldn’t be getting the No. 14 pick from Baltimore? How much does the $35M hit damage their plans for the rest of free agency, which seemingly would have prioritized addressing the offensive side of the ball further?

Those are all questions for the Raiders front office. We don’t know exactly what the motivations were for the Ravens in all this, but we do know that they seriously damaged the Raiders by backing out of this deal, and seemingly have very weak justification for it. The NFL and the NFLPA should demand to information from Baltimore to better understand if there was any new information that led to failing Crosby on his physical, or if this was just a case of getting cold feet and finding an excuse.

If it’s the latter, there should be major penalties for the Ravens in subverting the standard medical process to leverage it for their own gain. It’s just not fair to hurt a player and a team the way the Ravens have done.

title: Daniel Jones, Colts finalizing 2-year deal worth up to $100 million: Sources - The Athletic
description:
Veteran quarterback Daniel Jones and the Indianapolis Colts are finalizing a two-year contract worth up to $100 million with a base value of $88 million, league sources told The Athletic on Wednesday.
He signed a one-year, $14 million deal to join the Colts in free agency last year and quickly revived his career.
Jones, who turns 29 in May, finished the 2025 season with 19 touchdown passes and eight interceptions in 13 games.
How he fits Jones’ new deal solidifies his status as the starting quarterback moving forward.
Assuming Richardson joins another team in 2026, the Colts will need to add a third QB via the draft or free agency.
text:
Veteran quarterback Daniel Jones and the Indianapolis Colts are finalizing a two-year contract worth up to $100 million with a base value of $88 million, league sources told The Athletic on Wednesday. Jones was ranked fourth in The Athletic’s top 150 free agents and was the top-ranked QB.

“I’d love to be back here,” Jones said in January. “I’ve enjoyed being here, working with the coaching staff here, with the players. I think it’s a great organization and I’ve enjoyed being here. Obviously, there’s a business side to it, and I understand that, but I’ve really enjoyed being here around these people.”

A two-year deal between Jones and the Colts gives both sides ample flexibility moving forward. If Jones, who’s rehabbing from a torn right Achilles tendon, performs well over the next two seasons, he’ll be in line for his third massive payday when his contract expires. If Jones does not play well, however, the Colts, who do not have a first-round pick over the next two years, would be able to hit an organizational reset and perhaps draft a QB when they regain control of their first-round selection in 2028.

Jones was the No. 6 pick in 2019 by the New York Giants and led them to the playoffs in 2022, but his success was short-lived as the Giants eventually released him in 2024. He signed a one-year, $14 million deal to join the Colts in free agency last year and quickly revived his career. Jones beat out Anthony Richardson Sr., who recently requested a trade, for the starting job and led the Colts to an 8-2 start that placed them atop the AFC standings.

Jones was playing at a Pro Bowl level through the first half of the season, but his performance took a significant dip after he fractured his left fibula. The QB kept playing through that injury, but his mobility was compromised. Jones suffered a season-ending torn right Achilles tendon in early December. He said in January that he intended to be back in action by training camp in July.

Jones, who turns 29 in May, finished the 2025 season with 19 touchdown passes and eight interceptions in 13 games. He also tied for the Colts’ second-highest completion percentage (68.0) in a season and scored five rushing touchdowns.

Jones proved he could play winning football again if he’s in the right system, but injuries continue to hold him back. He has completed only one full NFL season in his first seven.

Jones’ return was a key factor in the Colts’ ability to retain Alec Pierce, who was arguably the best free-agent receiver and wanted a clear answer at QB before he chose a team. Pierce and Jones developed a strong bond on and off the field in their one season together, and now they’ll have the chance to build on it for the foreseeable future.

How he fits

Jones’ new deal solidifies his status as the starting quarterback moving forward.

Last season, he helped the team win its first season opener since 2013, improve to 2-0 for the first time since 2009 and climb to 8-2 atop the AFC standings. The Colts averaged 3.17 points per drive through their first 10 games, the fourth most by a team in its first 10 games this century, per TruMedia.

Jones was a vital part of Indy’s early offensive dominance, but the elephant in the room is his lengthy injury history. He has not played a full season since 2022 and is coming off arguably the most significant injury of his career, a ruptured Achilles tendon.

“It’s valid and it’s fair (to question Jones’ durability),” general manager Chris Ballard said at the combine. “He’s played 83 games in his career. The two big ones have been the ACL and the Achilles. I just think the way he prepares, how he’s built, I think in the long run Daniel is gonna be perfectly fine.”

2026 roster impact

Jones remains Indy’s starting quarterback ahead of Richardson and 2025 sixth-round pick Riley Leonard, though the team’s depth chart is likely to change because Richardson recently requested a trade.

Indianapolis plans to work with Richardson, who is entering the final year of his rookie deal and turns 24 in May, to find a suitable trade partner, league sources said.

Richardson started just 15 games in his first three years with the Colts, and there’s plenty of blame to go around – namely Richardson, Ballard and coach Shane Steichen – when explaining why Richardson failed in Indianapolis.

Assuming Richardson joins another team in 2026, the Colts will need to add a third QB via the draft or free agency.

Salary-cap update

The exact salary-cap implications of Jones’ new contract are unclear, but his 2026 cap hit will likely be lower than when the team placed the $37.8 million transition tag on him. Since the tag is technically a one-year, fully guaranteed deal, all of that money immediately counted toward the team’s cap space.

Now that Jones has a multiyear contract, Indianapolis can spread Jones’ cap hit across multiple seasons — probably lowering it for this upcoming season — so that it can maintain enough flexibility to address other roster deficiencies. The Colts still need to bolster their linebacker and defensive line units.

James Boyd’s takeaway

The Colts never had a Plan B at quarterback, especially after trading their 2026 and 2027 first-round picks — essentially two chances to draft a franchise QB — to the New York Jets in November for star cornerback Sauce Gardner.

Indy’s top goal this offseason was to run it back with Jones at the helm, believing that their 8-2 start last season wasn’t “a mirage,” according to Ballard.

Understandably, Indianapolis didn’t want to pursue a different quarterback after several years of turnover. However, the team is still betting a lot on a half-season of good play from Jones. The veteran will be just nine months removed from tearing his Achilles when the 2026 season begins, and he can’t be trusted to stay healthy.

title: NFL Free Agency: Four intriguing player-team pairings formed on day two - PFF
description:
Previously, we broke down some interesting fits from the first day of free agency.
Here are a handful of the most intriguing signings made on the first day of the 2026 legal tampering period, all of which can be found on PFF’s Free Agency Tracker.

text:
The Patriots improved the trenches and wide receiver corps: Alijah Vera-Tucker brings a documented injury history but has been excellent as a guard, and Romeo Doubs is poised to replace Stefon Diggs' production against man coverage.

The Texans' O-line rebuild continues: Braden Smith, the team's latest offensive line acquisition, has never earned a PFF pass-blocking grade below 67.8 in any of his eight NFL seasons. He also let up just one sack across his 13 starts in 2025.

2026 NFL Draft season is here: Try the best-in-class PFF Mock Draft Simulator and learn about 2026's top prospects while trading and drafting for your favorite NFL team.

The NFL’s free agency period brings a whirlwind of activity throughout the league as teams attempt to accrue talent and find the right fits for their roster. Some fits, whether they be analytically or schematically driven, are particularly interesting and can pay major dividends. Previously, we broke down some interesting fits from the first day of free agency.

Here are a handful of the most intriguing signings made on the first day of the 2026 legal tampering period, all of which can be found on PFF’s Free Agency Tracker.

New England Patriots: G Alijah Vera-Tucker (3 years, $42 million)

The Patriots vastly improved their offensive line last offseason by bringing in four new starters to play alongside lone incumbent Mike Onwenu, who himself moved back to his natural right guard position full time. Even accounting for the team's struggles in the postseason, those acquisitions allowed the Patriots’ offensive line to jump from 32nd in PFF overall grade in 2024 all the way up to 13th in 2025.

New England clearly isn’t yet satisfied with itstrench play despite making the Super Bowl. The front office has now made a big acquisition in the form of ex-New York Jets guard Alijah Vera-Tucker, who played mostly right guard for the Jets but slotted in at left guard for the entirety of his rookie season and has more than enough versatility, including 349 career snaps at tackle, to fill in wherever the Patriots need him.

Vera-Tucker’s production, when healthy, is hard to deny. If we exclude his 349 snaps at tackle, where he still played at a respectable level, Vera-Tucker earned a 75.4 PFF overall grade from 2021 to 2024. That ranked him 17th out of 103 guards who played at least 1,000 snaps in that span.

The primary issue with Vera-Tucker has been his inability to stay healthy. He missed all of 2025 due to a torn triceps, the same injury that curtailed his 2022 season. He also missed most of 2023 due to a torn Achilles. In total, he has played at least 500 snaps in just two of his five NFL seasons.

If he remains healthy for at least the majority of 2026, this deal could be a huge win for the Patriots, especially given that it moves Jared Wilson back to his natural position at center. If Vera-Tucker once again proves less than durable, New England could struggle up front.

New England Patriots: WR Romeo Doubs (4 years, $80 million)

The Patriots weren’t done after signing Alijah Vera-Tucker, adding former Green Bay Packer Romeo Doubs to their wide receiver unit. Doubs is coming off a season in which he posted a career-high 77.3 PFF receiving grade. He earned at least an 80.0 PFF receiving grade in his final three games while racking up 124 yards, the second-highest total of his career, in Green Bay’s playoff loss to the Chicago Bears.

Doubs enters New England as the direct replacement for the recently released Stefon Diggs, whose importance to Drake Maye and the Patriots’ offense can’t be overstated, particularly because of his ability to beat man coverage. He ranked 10th among qualified wide receivers with an 87.1 PFF receiving grade versus man coverage in 2025. He caught 78.1% of his targets against man coverage, which ranked fifth among the same group.

Doubs’ production against man coverage doesn’t quite match Diggs', but he is also no slouch. Over the past two seasons, Doubs has earned a solid 78.9 PFF receiving grade versus man coverage, averaging 1.78 yards per route run in the process — a tad behind Diggs’ 1.98 mark.

The 25-year-old will get a chance to prove he can consistently dominate one-on-one matchups as potentially the most heavily featured wide receiver in New England. His success in doing so will determine whether this is a good signing.

Washington Commanders: LB Leo Chenal (3 years, $24.75 million)

The Commanders entered this offseason with a desperate need to get more talented and younger on defense. Their best defensive player, Bobby Wagner, is currently a free agent. While Wagner could continue to help the team, he will enter the 2026 season at 36 years old, and his coverage ability has dropped off in recent years.

Washington appears to have chosen former Kansas City Chief Leo Chenal as Wagner’s replacement to build a more sustainable defense. Chenal has earned an 86.3 PFF overall grade and a 91.2 PFF run-defense grade through the first four seasons of his career. Both of those marks rank fifth among linebackers who have played at least 1,000 snaps in that span. Wagner is one of the very few players ahead of him in both categories.

Chenal could potentially help the Commanders improve in coverage, though. Known as more of an early-down thumper during his first two seasons, he has earned a solid 74.1 PFF coverage grade since the start of 2024. Washington’s linebacker unit notched just a 43.2 PFF coverage grade in 2025, 10th worst in the NFL.

Chenal’s all-around talent and ability to play on the line of scrimmage in certain fronts make him a very valuable piece of Washington’s defense, particularly at a price tag of just over $8 million per year. The only question is whether he can do it across a starter’s workload, as he has yet to play at least 600 snaps in any single season.

Houston Texans: T Braden Smith (2 years, $20 million)

Houston’s offensive line will once again be heavily scrutinized this season, as it has been the team’s biggest weakness over the past couple of years. The Texans ranked just 23rd in team PFF pass-blocking grade and traded their highest-graded pass-blocking lineman, Tytus Howard, to the Browns. They managed to re-sign guard Ed Ingram and tackle Trent Brown, but those two found more success in the run game than in pass blocking this past season.

Brown is now unlikely to start at right tackle after the team’s acquisition of former Indianapolis Colt Braden Smith, who has never earned a PFF pass-blocking grade below 67.8 in any of his eight NFL seasons. He also let up just one sack across his 13 starts in 2025.

Durability has been Smith’s primary issue over the past three seasons, as he’s missed 16 out of a possible 51 games. Brown’s presence as insurance alleviates that issue a bit. Smith’s two-year commitment to Houston could also take an offensive tackle out of consideration for the team in the first round of this year’s draft. It seems more likely that the Texans will further shore up the interior of the offensive or defensive lines with that pick.

title: A few Bengals thoughts leading into NFL tampering Day 3
description:
“This last half of the season, it’s been much better in terms of consistency,” Brown said.
They need help and experience at that position, and now there’s no sure thing at the linebacker position on the free agent market.
But he doesn’t solve the linebacker position for the Bengals.
*** The comparison to the 2020 offseason, not the 2021 offseason, feels even more solidified as the Bengals wait to address linebacker.
Because of the premium the Bengals are paying on pass rush depth, the Mafe signing shouldn’t take anyone off the board.
text:
Trey Hendrickson signing with the Baltimore Ravens is a reminder of how big of a year this is for Cincinnati Bengals left tackle Orlando Brown Jr.

Brown signed with the Bengals in 2023, and the expectation was that he’d win a few division titles and make a few Pro Bowls.

Brown has been one of the best leaders in years at Paycor Stadium, and he sets a tone with his energy and his toughness. But he’d tell you that he hasn’t played up to his own expectations.

He assessed his 2023 season as “ok,” and the year was impacted by a groin injury that impacted him. He played his best football with the Bengals during the first half of the 2024 season, but a leg injury forced him to miss time and had him playing “on one leg” when he was back in the lineup.

Here’s what Brown said at the end of 2025 about his third season with the Bengals.

“This last half of the season, it’s been much better in terms of consistency,” Brown said. “It’s been a pretty good year in pass protection. I look forward to continue to grow as a football player. The way I see it, I don’t want to be playing my best football until it’s time for me to walk away from this game. Hopefully, that means I’ve got a lot more time left. In January, I’ll sit down and reevaluate each game. I’ll look at it from a front office or coach’s perspective.”

Brown (entering a contract year) is still a great value on his contract, and he’s receiving what average left tackles are making. Still, a Bengals’ offense that’s now going to face Myles Garrett and Trey Hendrickson twice a year would really benefit from getting that fully healthy season where Brown consistently plays at the ceiling that he’s capable of reaching.

Brown becomes one of the most important Bengals in 2026. He has to be a twitchier, more dynamic pass protector against speed around the edge.

***

I’ve changed my mind on linebacker since the news hit last night that Kaden Ellis and Leo Chenal were off the board. My initial take was wrong.

The reality is that if Duke Tobin said at the combine that the Bengals had the resources to improve the linebacker position, there’s no “plan” for this spring that shouldn’t including improving that position in a meaningful way.

Barrett Carter and Demetrius Knight Jr. belong in roles in 2026. The Bengals shouldn’t give up on them. Their speed, range, ability to defeat blocks in space and upside in coverage should lead to them getting snaps in 2026. But even the Bengals were admitting at the end of the year that Carter and Knight needed a veteran alongside of them. Duke Tobin outlined that type of veteran at the combine, discussing a combo linebacker who could set the edge and blitz. It was a perfect description of Ellis and Chenal, who looked especially affordable.

Every week, I chart splash plays (positive and negative) for the Bengals’ defense. Teams were exploiting the Bengals’ linebackers last year. They need help and experience at that position, and now there’s no sure thing at the linebacker position on the free agent market.

There are vets like Lavonte David and Bobby Wagner who have lost a step and don’t fit the combo description. Dre Greenlaw has played in nine games over the last two years. Devin White and Shaq Thompson were playing on the veteran minimum last year.

My favorite fit left is Akeem Davis-Gaither. I studied his game before the Bengals played the Cardinals in Week 17. He was very serviceable in Arizona, and he has specific experience in the combo role the Bengals are looking for. He’s also a proven leader who’s very well respected around the building.

ADG checks the combo box. He’d prevent Knight from having to set the edge, which is good. But he doesn’t solve the linebacker position for the Bengals. And that’s a spot that it really felt like the Bengals wanted to solve heading into the offseason.

Did they end up undervaluing that position? Did they just not like Ellis and Chenal, even though they’ve been successful combo linebackers in different schemes? Did they feel like they didn’t have much left to spend after aggressive, big deals for Boye Mafe and Bryan Cook?

Whatever the reason is, it’s something we’ll look back to when offensive coordinators scheme up ways to test the Bengals’ linebackers in 2026.

***

The comparison to the 2020 offseason, not the 2021 offseason, feels even more solidified as the Bengals wait to address linebacker.

It feels like the Bengals just aren’t going to be able to fill every hole this offseason.

They still need a backup quarterback, a swing tackle, more edge depth, multiple pass rushing defensive tackles, a linebacker, a starting slot corner, a depth cover corner and safety depth.

I like the big moves for Cook and Mafe. The flip side of that is that you don’t check every box without very creative cap math (so far, that’s been no dice for the Bengals). So far, the Bengals have opted for impact players at positions of need as opposed to filling every single need.

The Bengals’ defensive rebuild started in earnest in Week 6 last year when they benched Logan Wilson. The problem is this rebuild is coinciding with this pivot point in the Joe Burrow window. They’re in this position because of how many unsuccessful personnel decisions were made between 2022 and 2025.

They’re probably just not going to land that pass rushing defensive tackle — it doesn’t exist in free agency anymore or the first round. They’ll probably end up counting on rookies to be important depth at a few positions. They’ll be counting on their first-round pick to be one of the best players on the defense.

One of the defining decisions of free agency for them was Boye Mafe over John Franklin-Myers. Even though Franklin-Myers filled a greater team need at a position they haven’t been able to fill for five years, Mafe is simply the better player and the bigger swing for more upside. Because they signed Mafe, the defense won’t be perfect. But Mafe can give you more splash negative plays than JFM.

***

One of the most memorable plays from the 2025 season was the one that effectively lost them the Bills game. Ced Johnson was unblocked and had Josh Allen dead to rights. Johnson whiffed, and Allen ran for a touchdown.

Signing Mafe has nothing to do with the role of Shemar Stewart. Plays from depth edge rushers can shape your season. There will be plenty of ways to get Stewart on the field, especially because of his potential as an interior rusher.

What will really shape Stewart’s 2026 season is how he approaches this offseason program and training camp.

“He needs a big offseason,” defensive line coach Jerry Montgomery said in January. “He needs a tremendous strength and conditioning (program) in the offseason. It’s got to be something he wants, not that I want. He has the size and strength to do whatever he wants. It’s just the willingness to want to learn and grow and be locked into the things we’re asking him to do. He can do whatever he wants. He can move all around. But it’s got to be something that he’s passionate about. If it’s not, then he’ll have to beat out other people on edges.”

***

The start to free agency so far should have almost no impact on the Bengals’ draft strategy.

They could draft Caleb Downs and slide him right into the slot.

They could draft Rueben Bain or David Bailey and give them 50% of the snaps right away (Stewart would still get plenty of playing time in that scenario).

It’d be a risk to throw yet another rookie into a linebacker room that’s already too inexperienced, but you could draft Sonny Styles and scheme up ways to showcase his athleticism in the middle of the defense.

You could reach for a defensive tackle like Peter Woods if he blows you away in the pre-draft process.

You could even draft an offensive tackle to add to your pipeline.

Because of the premium the Bengals are paying on pass rush depth, the Mafe signing shouldn’t take anyone off the board. Signing Cook makes it tougher to go out and get a true free safety, but Downs isn’t a true free safety.

The Bengals still need to do more over the next few days to give themselves the luxury of drafting the best player available. Since they still need starters at linebacker and slot corner and important depth at tackle, outside corner and quarterback, they’re a few moves away from being able to avoid the pigeonhole they were in during the middle rounds of the draft last season.

title: Daniel Jones contract: Colts QB agrees to two-year deal during NFL free agency
description:
The Colts and Daniel Jones hve agreed to a two-year, $88 million deal that keeps him in Indianapolis and takes him off of the transition tag, CBS Sports NFL insider Jonathan Jones confirmed.
Jones, 28, and the Colts continued to work on a multiyear deal even after he received the rarely used transition tag.
After the Giants released him midway through the 2025 season, Jones landed on the Vikings' practice squad.
It looked like the former first-round pick, who signed a four-year, $160 million deal in New York just two years earlier, would become a journeyman.
Then he signed a one-year, $14 million deal with the Colts and the chance to beat out Anthony Richardson for the starting job.
text:
The Colts and Daniel Jones hve agreed to a two-year, $88 million deal that keeps him in Indianapolis and takes him off of the transition tag, CBS Sports NFL insider Jonathan Jones confirmed. The deal also includes a host of incentives, with a max value of $100 million.

Jones, 28, and the Colts continued to work on a multiyear deal even after he received the rarely used transition tag. The tag would have paid him $37.833 million for the 2026 season -- roughly $6 million less than the franchise tag -- while allowing him to receive offers from other teams. The Colts still would have had a chance to match any outside deal he agreed to.

Instead, he's staying right where his remarkable bounce-back season occurred, and he gets a lot of money to do so. That includes $50 million in the first year of the deal, more than $13 more than he would have made on the transition tag. Overall, the $44 million per year is the 18th-most for a quarterback currently.

After the Giants released him midway through the 2025 season, Jones landed on the Vikings' practice squad. It looked like the former first-round pick, who signed a four-year, $160 million deal in New York just two years earlier, would become a journeyman.

Then he signed a one-year, $14 million deal with the Colts and the chance to beat out Anthony Richardson for the starting job. He took advantage of that opportunity and ran with it, winning the job and playing extraordinarily well. The Colts started 8-2. Then a slide for both Jones and the team began: He suffered a fractured tibia -- which he played through -- and then a season-ending torn Achilles a few weeks later. The Colts finished 8-9 and missed the playoffs.

Still, Jones finished third in net yards per attempt (7.7), sixth in completion percentage (68%) and ninth in passer rating (100.2) in his first year with the Colts. He was first in expected points added per play. More than the numbers, Jones showed improved playmaking ability, using his athleticism to his advantage and making several high-level throws in clutch moments while operating Shane Steichen's offense with aplomb.

The Colts, who also spent big on wide receiver Alec Pierce but lost wide receiver Michael Pittman Jr. via trade in the process, will now hope Jones can recover from his latest significant injury and return to the form he was in to start the 2025 season. Indianapolis still has star running back Jonathan Taylor, a strong offensive line and tight end Tyler Warren coming off an impressive rookie season. The healthy returns of cornerbacks Sauce Gardner and Charvarius Ward should also be a boon for the defense.

For more 2026 NFL free agency coverage:

Live updates: Latest rumors, signings and updates

Team-by-team signings tracker: Full list of every transaction this offseason

Team grades: How has your favorite squad fared?

Grades for biggest moves: Assessing deals from Day 1 and Day 2

Winners and losers: Players and teams that defined Day 1 and Day 2

Why the deal makes sense for both sides

Deals require compromise. Money isn't unlimited. The player pool is limited. The Colts wanted to have Jones back, which is why they applied the transition tag. But Jones wanted to make more money, which is why the two sides kept working. The result has good points for both sides.

Jones will like the significant raise in 2026, even coming off a major injury. He'll also like the relative security of a two-year deal; if he had played well on the transition tag, the Colts could have tried to use another tag on him in 2027. And while getting that second year is nice, it's not prohibitive. If Jones plays well over the next two years, he'll be a free agent again at just 30 years old, ready for another big pay day.

On the Colts' side, the Colts is big but not outlandish. This is what it costs for a starting quarterback, and the Colts seemed to have found a prime reclamation project success before Jones' injury last year. Should he be able to return to the level of play he maintained early last year, this could be a bargain, in fact. Only $50 million of the $88 million is guaranteed, per ESPN's Adam Schefter. If Jones struggles, Indianapolis isn't completely stuck.

Is Colts WR Alec Pierce worth his $116 million contract? Here's how he could live up to it

Jared Dubin

Are Pierce, Jones worth it?

That's the pair of questions the Colts will be asking themselves -- and everyone will be asking about the Colts -- all offseason. My colleague Jared Dubin broke down that question regarding Pierce. Paying Pierce as a top-10 receiver while getting objectively not top-10 receiver production is putting a lot of faith in Pierce's improvement continuing. And he made a big improvement in 2025: the first player to hit 1,000 yards on 20+ yards per catch since 2019. In a league that places a premium on explosive plays, Pierce delivers them. Still, he has mostly been a role player, even in 2025, when he was a very good role player. Add in the Pittman departure, and the Colts also have to find another perimeter wide receiver opposite Pierce.

Jones, meanwhile, might be an even more convoluted case. After two poor seasons plagued by poor health in New York following his strong 2022, Jones looked like a renewed version of himself in 2025. He pushed the ball downfield with unprecedented rates and efficiency. He avoided sacks and pressure at the best rate in his career. His 8.1 yards per attempt was 1.3 yards better than his previous high.

Can he get back to that? Achilles tears are no easy ailment to overcome, and Jones' mobility was a big part of his success in Steichen's offense, which requires under-center, play-action and rollout concepts. Plus, he'll be down his toughest wide receiver in Pittman. Every recovery is different, so evaluating a player's projected recovery is fool's errand. Still, an immediate return to his prior form seems unlikely, and his injury history has to be considered, too.

What's next for Indianapolis?

The internal matters -- Jones and Pierce -- were far and away Indianapolis' biggest priorities. What now?

The Colts had been in on the Trey Hendrickson sweepstakes -- eventually won by the Ravens -- as well, so perhaps an upgrade at EDGE is in order, even after addign Arden Key. The Colts lost Kwity Paye to the Raiders, and they desperate need some defensive disruptors. They also lost Braden Smith to the Texans, so the offensive line has one hole to take care of, whether internally or externally. Add in the Pittman departure, the Zaire Franklin departure and an impending Nick Cross exit, and there's plenty of work to do on both sides of the ball, especially without a first-round pick.

That's not to mention that Indianapolis needs a quarterback who can start should Jones be unable to begin the year. Is that Riley Leonard? Is that Richardson, who got permission to seek a trade? Is that an external addition?
